ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pomfret entry; no specific entry for wild-caught Chinese pomfret with bone. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers Pampus spp. broadly; no bone-in or wild-caught sub-facet available. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 covers pomfret as a food substance; no specific wild-caught or bone-in variant. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of wild-caught Chinese pomfret with bone is approximately 150 g of bone-in fillet, as commonly served in a Chinese seafood hot pot or steamed fish dish.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, pomfret, raw (Pampus chinensis) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw pomfret and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Pampus chinensis. Values represent raw, bone-in fish meat per 100 g edible portion. The bone-in presentation affects yield but not per-100g edible nutrient density materially. Sodium reflects natural fish sodium without added salt.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fish and Seafood — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure fish and seafood contain negligible digestible carbohydrate and have an effectively zero glycemic response. No direct GI test exists for Chinese pomfret specifically. By analogy with all tested fish and seafood in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values near zero due to absence of glycemic carbohydrates, GI is set to 1 (lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 150 g serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low natural sodium content (~72 mg per 100 g) and high potassium (~340 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Wild-caught Chinese pomfret is naturally low in sodium and rich in potassium, making it a suitable protein source for individuals managing hypertension, provided it is prepared without added salt, soy sauce, or high-sodium marinades.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrates and high-quality lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chinese pomfret contains no digestible carbohydrates and has a negligible glycemic response, making it an excellent protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids also support insulin sensitivity and cardiovascular health.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, low saturated fat, and high lean protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wild-caught Chinese pomfret provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (approximately 0.6 g per 100 g) and low saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g), supporting a cardioprotective dietary pattern; regular consumption of oily fish is associated with reduced cardiovascular disease risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wild Caught Chinese Pomfret with Bone (野生白鲳鱼片（有骨）) is a premium marine fish dish featuring bone-in sliced fillets of Pampus chinensis, the Chinese silver pomfret, sourced from wild-caught stocks. Chinese pomfret is prized in Chinese and Southeast Asian cuisine for its delicate, sweet, and mildly rich flavour with a tender, fine-textured white flesh. The bone-in presentation is traditional and is considered to enhance flavour during cooking. It is commonly served in Chinese seafood hot pot (火锅), steamed whole or as fillets with ginger and soy, or braised in sauces. In Singapore, it is a sought-after seafood item at Chinese seafood restaurants and hot pot establishments. Nutritionally, Chinese pomfret is a lean, high-protein fish providing approximately 18.5 g protein and only 2.4 g fat per 100 g, with an energy density of approximately 96 kcal per 100 g. It contains omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and phosphorus. It contains no digestible carbohydrates and has a negligible glycemic index, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The fish is naturally low in sodium and free from gluten and dairy. Wild-caught status is associated with higher omega-3 content compared to farmed equivalents. The bone-in presentation requires care during consumption but is preferred by many diners for its superior taste.
common_user_queries: Is white pomfret high in sodium?
Can I eat pomfret if I have high blood pressure?
Wild caught pomfret vs farmed pomfret nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for raw Pampus chinensis. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for wild-caught Chinese pomfret with bone was identified. Values are cross-referenced and consistent across sources but represent an adaptation rather than an exact match. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database fish category data.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — raw fish contains no digestible carbohydrates per USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA data. 2. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — no sugars present in raw fish flesh. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — fish contains no dietary fibre. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — fish is not a source of vitamin C. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — naturally occurring trans fats in fish are negligible and reported as zero in USDA SR Legacy. 6. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this fish contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for all fish and seafood. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 0.8 g from USDA SR Legacy pomfret analogue). 8. polyunsaturated_fat_g estimated at 0.6 g from USDA SR Legacy pomfret analogue. 9. The food is served bone-in; per-100g values reflect edible flesh portion. 10. Wild-caught status noted from input source (GrabFood Singapore, Hai Kah Lang restaurant). 11. Halal status: fish is generally considered halal in Islamic dietary law; no pork or alcohol ingredients. 12. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03411433 is the closest parent for pomfret; no exact wild-caught bone-in entry exists. 13. FoodEx2 code A00LC is the closest parent for Pampus spp. in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
